Pre-Lesson • COC 1 Introduction

Before You Start COC 1 — Know a basic knowledge!

Before getting into Computer System Servicing, it is essential that you know and understand the parts and components of a computer, Tools and Maintenace, Occupational Health and Safety, and The 5s Educating yourself with the basic functions helps you understand how the computer works — and will help you diagnose problems in the future (covered in COC 4).

Upon completing COC 1, you are expected to:
Identify computer parts and their functions
Assemble and disassemble a computer system
Perform OS installation, BIOS config & drive partitioning
Install third-party apps, drivers & peripherals
Pre-Lesson Progress0%

This page covers everything you need to know before entering Core Competency 1. Think of it as your foundation — without knowing your computer parts, the rest of CSS becomes very hard to follow.

After finishing this intro, you'll be fully ready to tackle COC 1 → Install & Configure with confidence. And don't worry — components like advanced troubleshooting are saved for COC 4. Let's focus here first!

Tools and Maintenance

📋
Contents of This Lesson

This lesson covers three topics: 1) Tools and Maintenance, 2) Occupational Health and Safety (OHS), and 3) The 5S Method. Master all three before proceeding to COC 1.

Hand Tools

Essential tools used in computer servicing

Phillips Screwdriver
Drives cross-head screws — most common type for PC builds
Flat-head Screwdriver
Single blade tip for slotted screws; also used for prying
Allen Wrench
Turns bolts and screws with hexagonal sockets (hex key)
Head Mount Flashlight
Portable hands-free electric light for working inside dark cases
Tweezers
Small tool for picking up objects too tiny to handle by hand
Pliers
Grips, bends, and cuts wires or small components
Long-nose Pliers
Extended jaws for reaching tight, narrow spaces inside the case
Cable Tie (Zip Tie)
Fastener for bundling and organizing cables; also called hose tie or zap strap
Crimping Tool
is also a key hand tool — it's used to attach connectors (RJ45/RJ11) onto network cables. You will use this heavily in COC 2 when making LAN cables with T568B wiring.

Cleaning Tools & Maintenance

Proper cleaning extends the life of every computer component

Cleaning
Remove dirt such as rust from hand tools. Can be done by washing, wiping, or brushing.
Lubricating
Apply grease or oil to ensure smooth movement and prevent rust from developing on moving parts.
📌
Maintaining Hand Tools, Equipment & Paraphernalia
  • Make it a habit to clean tools after use and before storage. Wipe with a dust-free rag.
  • Store tools properly in a toolbox, storage container, or shelving unit.
  • Lubricate moving parts using the specified type of oil to keep tools efficient.
  • Always operate tools according to the manufacturer's instructions.
  • Wear gloves, masks, and goggles when cleaning tools to protect from harmful agents.

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) & Safety Gear

Garments designed to protect the wearer from injury or infection

Personal Protective Equipment
Clothing, helmets, goggles, or other garments designed to protect the wearer's body from injury or infections
Anti-Static Wrist Strap
ESD wrist strap or ground bracelet — prevents static electricity buildup that can cause Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) damage
Anti-Static Mat
Grounded mat that dissipates static electricity — used together with the wrist strap when working on sensitive components

Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)

Policies and procedures that aim to protect health, safety and welfare at the workplace

⚠️
What is OHS?

Occupational Health and Safety refers to the legislation, policies, procedures and activities that aim to protect the health, safety and welfare of all people at the workplace. The goals of OHS programs include fostering a safe and healthy work environment and protecting co-workers, family members, employers, customers, and others who might be affected.

Common OHS Standards — Computer Servicing

  • › 1. Do not work alone so that there's someone who can take care of you in case of emergency.
  • › 2. Always power off the computer and unplug it before working on it.
  • › 3. Take away any liquid near your working area to avoid electrocution or accidentally damaging computer parts.
  • › 4. Be careful with tools that may cause short circuit.
  • › 5. Always ground or discharge yourself before touching any part of the computer.
  • › 6. Do not use excessive force if things don't quite slip into place.
  • › 7. Clean the area before and after using it to maintain sanitation and prevent accidents.
  • › 8. Hold the components on the edges and do not touch the Integrated Circuit (IC) parts.
  • › 9. Always wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) in accordance with the organization's OHS procedures and practices.
  • › 10. Make sure that the pins are properly aligned when connecting a cable connector.
  • › 11. Contingency measures during workplace accidents, fire and other emergencies are recognized.
  • › 12. Use brush, compressed air or blower in cleaning the computer system.
5️

The 5"S" Method

A workplace organization method using five Japanese words starting with "S"

🇯🇵
What is 5S?

5S is a foundational approach for lean manufacturing and efficient workspace organization. It's a habitual system that aims to create a clean, organized, and efficient workspace. It originates from five Japanese words all starting with "S".

1. Seiri — Sort
Suriin ayon sa
Remove unnecessary items from the workspace, keeping only what is needed for current operations.
2. Seiton — Set in Order
Kaayusan
Organize the remaining items, making them easy to find, use, and return to their proper location.
3. Seiso — Shine
Linisin / Pakintabin
Clean the workspace thoroughly — improves the environment and helps identify problems like leaks or equipment malfunctions.
4. Seiketsu — Standardize
Daloy
Create clear procedures and visual controls to maintain the improvements achieved in the previous three steps.
5. Shitsuke — Sustain
Gawi / Kasanayan
Make the 5S practices a habit, ensuring they are followed consistently and become a part of the company culture. This final step ensures the other four are maintained permanently.

HDD vs SSD — Which Is Better?

Understanding the difference is crucial for storage decisions.

Teacher's Note

When upgrading your storage, an SSD is almost always the best choice. The performance improvement is massive — a computer with an SSD will feel several times faster during startup and loading applications.

Feature HDD (Hard Disk Drive) SSD (Solid State Drive)
Technology Spinning magnetic platters with a mechanical read/write arm Flash memory chips (no moving parts)
Speed ~100–150 MB/s read speed ~500 MB/s (SATA) up to 7,000 MB/s (NVMe)
Durability Fragile — moving parts can fail from vibration or drops Very durable — no moving parts to break
Noise Produces clicking/spinning sounds Completely silent
Power Use Higher power consumption Lower power use → better battery life on laptops
Price per GB Cheaper (great for mass storage) More expensive, but prices are dropping fast
Best For Storing large files: movies, backups, archives Operating system, applications — daily use
Important for CSS Students

When installing an OS in COC 1, you'll choose which drive to install it on. Always install your OS on an SSD if available — it makes the system dramatically faster and more responsive.

Quick Reference Guide

A fast summary of all 10 computer parts and their purpose.

# Part Primary Function Type
1 Hardware The physical touchable, electronic and mechanical parts of a computer system. Internal
2 System Case Houses and protects all internal components; provides structure and airflow Internal
3 Motherboard Connects all components; contains expansion slots, I/O ports, and system clock Internal
4 Power Supply (PSU) Converts AC power to DC power; distributes power to all components Power
5 CPU Performs all calculations and processes instructions — the "brain" of the computer Processing
6 RAM Temporary working memory; holds open programs and data. Lost on power off Processing
7 HDD / SSD Permanent data storage; holds OS, files, and applications Storage
8 CPU Cooler Dissipates heat from the CPU using a heatsink and fan (or liquid cooling) Cooling
9 Graphics Card (GPU) Handles all graphical processing; outputs video to monitors Processing
10 Peripherals Input: keyboard, mouse, mic, camera. Output: monitor, speakers External
11 Peopleware Refers to people involved in the data processing operations such as the system administrator, office workers, students and others. Connectivity
12 Wireless Network Card Enables the computer to connect to Wi-Fi networks Connectivity
13 Peopleware Refers to people involved in the data processing operations such as the system administrator, office workers, students and others. Connectivity
🎓
Study Tip

When memorizing these parts, group them into categories: Processing (CPU, RAM, GPU), Storage (HDD/SSD), Power (PSU), Structure (Case, Motherboard), Cooling (CPU Cooler), and I/O (Peripherals, Wi-Fi). This grouping method makes it much easier to recall during exams!

Assembling and Disassembling a PC

Core Module 1 — Fundamentals of Computer Hardware & Networking

What is Computer Assembly?

Computer Assembly is a process in which all the internal components required for the computer system are fitted so as to make the computer functional. There is a proper sequence of attachment of each and every component into the computer system.

▶️
📹 How to Assemble a PC — Step-by-Step Video Guide
Watch the full tutorial to reinforce your understanding
Materials Required for Assembly

Before you begin, make sure you have all the necessary components ready:

Computer Case (with PSU installed)
Motherboard
CPU (Processor)
Heat Sink / Fan Assembly
Thermal Compound (Thermal Paste)
RAM Module(s)
Motherboard Standoffs and Screws
Anti-static Wrist Strap & Mat
Tool Kit (screwdrivers, pliers)
Internal Drives (HDD / SSD)

Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure

1

Open The Case

Remove the side panel to access the interior
▼

The first step in assembling a computer is to open the computer case. To open the case, first remove the screws of the left side cover and slide the side cover off. This exposes the interior where all components will be installed.

Make sure to place the screws in a container so they don't get lost. Lay the case flat on your anti-static mat before proceeding.

Safety: Wear anti-static wrist strap
2

Install The Power Supply (PSU)

Secure the PSU in the designated bay
▼

The next step is to install the Power Supply Unit. Position the PSU in the PSU bay (usually at the top or bottom-rear of the case). There are usually four screws that attach the power supply to the case — tighten them firmly but avoid overtightening.

Route the cables through the case cable management holes if available, to keep things tidy for later steps.

Note: Fan faces toward ventilation
3

Attach Components To The Motherboard

Install CPU, thermal paste, heat sink/fan, and RAM
▼

The motherboard must be prepared before installation. Do this outside the case on your anti-static mat:

  • Install the CPU: A CPU socket uses a series of pins to connect the processor to the motherboard. Align the CPU with the socket (match the triangle markers), gently drop it in, and secure the retention arm.
  • Apply Thermal Compound: Place a small pea-sized amount of thermal paste on the center of the CPU. This fills microscopic gaps between the CPU and cooler for better heat transfer.
  • Install Heat Sink & Fan (HSF): The HSF is an active cooling solution used to cool down the CPU. Seat the heatsink over the CPU and secure it with its mounting screws/clips. Connect the fan power cable to the CPU_FAN connector on the motherboard.
  • Install RAM: First ensure compatibility with the motherboard (DDR4, DDR5, etc.). Press the RAM stick down firmly into the slot until the side clips click into place.
CPU: Match triangle corner RAM: Match the notch position
4

Install The Motherboard

Mount the prepared motherboard into the case
▼

After preparing the motherboard with CPU, cooler, and RAM, you can install it in the computer case.

  • First, install the I/O shield (backplate) into the rear cutout of the case.
  • Install motherboard standoffs into the case at the appropriate mounting holes — these raise the motherboard off the case to prevent short circuits.
  • Align the motherboard with the standoffs and the I/O shield, then secure it with screws.
Standoffs prevent short circuits
5

Install Internal Drives

Mount HDD or SSD in the drive bays
▼

The hard drive is the device which stores all the data. Slide the drive into the appropriate drive bay and secure it with screws.

  • Connect the SATA Data Cable from the HDD/SSD socket to the motherboard SATA socket.
  • Connect the Power Cable coming from the SMPS (PSU) to the power socket of the Hard Disk Drive.
  • For M.2 SSDs: Insert at an angle into the M.2 slot and secure with a single screw.
SATA data cable → motherboard Power cable → PSU
6

Connect All Internal Cables

Power and data cables throughout the system
▼

Power Cables distribute electricity from the PSU to the motherboard and all components. Data Cables transmit data between the motherboard and storage devices such as hard drives.

  • Connect front panel headers (power button, reset, LEDs) to the motherboard's front panel header pins.
  • Connect USB 3.0 and audio headers from the case to the motherboard.
  • Connect any GPU power cables if a dedicated graphics card is installed.
Cable management = better airflow
7

Install Motherboard Power Connections

ATX 24-pin and CPU power connectors
▼

The ATX Main Power Connector (Advanced Technology Extended) will have either 20 or 24 pins — this is the primary power connection to the motherboard.

  • Connect the large 24-pin ATX connector to the matching port on the motherboard's right side.
  • Connect the smaller 4-pin or 8-pin EPS connector near the top of the motherboard — this powers the CPU directly.
24-pin = main power 4/8-pin = CPU power
8

Connect External Cables to the Computer

Monitor, keyboard, mouse, audio, and power
▼

Close the case side panel and connect all external peripherals:

  • Monitor: The VGA cable (or HDMI/DisplayPort) is used to connect the monitor to the video output port on the motherboard or GPU.
  • Keyboard: If it uses a USB connector, plug it into any USB port on the back of the computer.
  • Headphones / Speakers / Microphone: Connect to the audio ports (green = output, pink = microphone input).
  • Printer, Scanner, Webcam: Connect via USB ports or wirelessly.
  • Power Supply Cable: The power cable connects into the back of the computer case's PSU port.
VGA/HDMI = monitor Green jack = speakers/headphones
9

Boot The Computer For The First Time

Power on and verify POST (Power-On Self Test)
▼

Always remember that the first step is to push the power button of the CPU, then the monitor's. An Operating System or system software like Windows or Linux will start loading.

  • If POST succeeds, you'll hear a single beep and the system will boot.
  • Enter BIOS/UEFI (usually by pressing Del or F2) to verify components are detected.
  • Check that RAM, storage drives, and CPU are all recognized.
  • Proceed with OS installation once everything checks out.
🎉
Now Your Computer Is Ready To Use!

Congratulations — your assembled PC is ready for OS installation and configuration.

⚠️
What is Disassembly?

Disassembly is the process of breaking down a device into separate parts. Always follow proper OHS procedures before and during disassembly.

Materials Required for Disassembly
One Working PC
Anti-static Wrist Strap
Anti-static Mat
Anti-static Bags (various sizes)
Technician's Toolkit
Plastic cup/box for screws, nuts & bolts

🔨 Step-by-Step Disassembly Procedure

1

Unplugging

Disconnect all power and peripheral cables
▼
  • Unplug the power cord from the PC.
  • Unplug all peripherals attached to the computer, such as the keyboard, mouse, monitor, and headphones.
  • Press the power button once (with PC unplugged) to discharge residual electricity from capacitors.
Always power off first!
2

Open The Case

Remove side panel screws and slide the panel off
▼

First remove the screws of the left side cover and slide the side cover. Place the panel in a safe location where it won't be accidentally stepped on or scratched.

Tip: Place screws in a cup
3

Disconnect All Connectors

Remove SATA, power, and data cables
▼

Disconnect all the connectors connected to the motherboard. These include:

  • SATA power cable and data cable of HDD
  • SATA cable of Optical Drive
  • Front panel headers (power button, LEDs)
  • USB and audio headers
  • GPU power cables (if applicable)
Document connections before removing
4

Remove The Fan

Unscrew and detach the CPU cooling fan
▼

First disconnect the CPU fan cable from the CPU_FAN header on the motherboard. Then unscrew the fan/heatsink mounting screws (diagonal pattern to prevent warping). Gently twist the heatsink before lifting to break the thermal paste bond.

Twist gently — don't yank
5

Remove The Power Supply

Disconnect and unseat the PSU
▼

The power supply is connected to the motherboard by a 20-pin connector and 4-pin connector. To remove:

  • Press the clip on the 24-pin connector and pull straight out.
  • Disconnect the 4/8-pin CPU power connector.
  • Remove the 4 screws holding the PSU to the case and slide it out.
Store in anti-static bag
6

Remove HDD and Optical Drive

Unseat storage drives from bays
▼

Remove the SATA cable connecting to the HDD and Optical Drive in the motherboard. Unscrew the drive from its bay and slide it out carefully.

  • Store drives in anti-static bags to prevent static damage.
  • Handle by edges — avoid touching the circuit boards on HDDs.
Handle with care — data is fragile
7

Remove RAM (Random Access Memory)

Release retention clips and lift out modules
▼

To remove the RAM, push down on the retention clips at both ends of the RAM slot simultaneously. The RAM stick will pop up at an angle — pull it out carefully and store in an anti-static bag.

Push both clips simultaneously
8

Remove Expansion Cards

GPU and other PCIe cards
▼

To remove the expansion cards (like the GPU), disconnect any cables attached to it first. Remove the screw holding the card bracket to the case, then press the PCIe retention clip at the end of the slot and pull the card straight up.

Press PCIe clip to release
9

Remove Motherboard

Final step — unseat the main PCB
▼

To remove the motherboard, ensure all cables have been disconnected. Remove all motherboard screws (typically 6–9 screws). Carefully lift the motherboard out at an angle, clearing the I/O shield and standoffs. Place on anti-static mat.

Last component to remove
10

Reassemble The Components

Identify, photograph, and reassemble correctly
▼
  • Identify every component and take its photograph before reassembly.
  • After identification of each component, put all the components back in their place and ensure that all cables and wires are connected at the right place to avoid further troubleshooting.
📸
Pro Tip:

Always photograph cable connections BEFORE disconnecting them. This makes reassembly much easier!

Ready to Enter COC 1?

You've covered all the computer parts. You're now prepared to install, configure, and work with computer systems!
